title: Pairwise Liouvillian learning from randomized measurements: practical aspects and guidelines for operating the protocol in large-scale experiments
authors: William T. Lam, Manoj K. Joshi, Daniel Stilck França, Benoît Vermersch
abstract: We review and numerically study a protocol for Liouvillian learning based on randomized Pauli states and measurements. In particular, in the two-body, long-range interactions, and single-body noise setting, we describe the complete workflow to obtain the coefficients of the Liouvillian in an efficient and pairwise manner, meaning that the required classical memory is independent of the system size. We also provide guidelines for choosing the parameters for data acquisition and postprocessing that minimize the total reconstruction error.
summary: This paper presents a scalable method to reconstruct the Hamiltonian and dissipative components of a quantum system using randomized measurements. By focusing on pairwise interactions, the protocol avoids the exponential memory bottleneck typically associated with large-scale quantum system characterization. It also provides practical optimization guidelines to balance statistical and systematic errors during data processing.
main_problem: Developing a scalable and efficient protocol for Liouvillian learning to reconstruct the full dynamics (Hamiltonian and noise) of large-scale quantum systems without exponential classical memory costs.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a pairwise learning protocol where the required classical memory is independent of system size and the number of random settings scales logarithmically with N, providing optimal guidelines for minimizing reconstruction error.
method: The protocol uses randomized Pauli measurements and state preparation to extract local Liouvillian coefficients via polynomial interpolation of time-series data and least-squares solutions.
model_or_system: The study focuses on Analog Quantum Simulators (AQS) such as trapped ions or superconducting circuits, specifically modeling an XY model with power-law interactions and single-body Lindblad noise.
key_observables: Expectation values of single-body and two-body Pauli operators and their time derivatives at t=0.
important_parameters: Number of random settings (R), number of measurement repetitions (Nm), interpolation degree (Dl), and system size (N).
main_assumptions: The protocol assumes Markovian dynamics and a specific ansatz for the Liouplovian involving two-body interactions and single-body noise.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental workflow; Figure 2 shows the full-rank probability of the configuration matrix; Figure 4 shows time dynamics of observables; Figure 5 illustrates the bias-variance tradeoff in interpolation; Figure 6 shows reconstruction error saturation with system size.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Liouvillian learning problem, details the pairwise randomized measurement protocol, provides a mathematical framework for extracting coefficients, analyzes the scalability and error trade-offs, and validates the method with numerical simulations of an XY model.
why_interesting: It provides a practical, scalable framework for characterizing open quantum systems, which is essential for verifying the dynamics of large-scale quantum simulators and understanding decoherence in many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Pairwise Liouvillian learning experimental protocol. We prepare randomized Pauli states, time evolve the system and measure in random Pauli basis. We repeat the protocol for R random local gates {(Ui, Vi)}, for NT time points and NM repetitions. Then we extract the Liouvillian from the measured bitstrings.

Figure 2. Probability of having a full rank system for a given qubit pair, as a function of the number of random Pauli unitaries, calculated numerically with 1000 samples. We interpolated the data with a Gumbel function e−e−(R−R0)/µ, with R0 = 57.76 and µ = 15.0. After R > 200, the system is full rank with unity probability.

Figure 3. Probability of having a full rank system on all the qubit pairs, as a function of the number of random Pauli unitaries, calculated numerically with 1000 samples. We interpolated the isoprobability curve p(R, N) = 0.5, and ob- tained a logarithmic dependence R = ˜R0 + ˜µ log(N) of the parameters, with ˜R0, ˜µ = 39.31, 38.84. This illustrates the scalability of the full-rank condition with the system size.

Figure 5. Reconstruction error as a function of the maximal time and interpolation degree. We simu- lated numerically the protocol with the parameters N = 2, R = 1000, NM = 1000, NT = 40, with a trapped ion Hamilto- nian, and averaged the data over 20 samples. This illustrates a bias (low degree, long times) variance (high degree, short times) tradeoff.

Figure 4. Time dynamics of O(t) (left) and Y (t) (right). The model and parameters are given in Sec. V. Applying the inversion matrix ˜ M −1 to the measured observables O(t), we obtain a few curves Y (t) with reduced statistical noise and well-suited for polynomial interpolation.
